access.accessMethod: Read the pinned CME submodule file from the repository checkout.
access.entityLabel: General Corpus source XML for CME00007

rights.basis: The artifact represents the U-M item whose provider asserts No Copyright - United States; no separate rights statement was found in the local wrapper.
rights.component: textual content of the local CME00007 XML snapshot

entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00007
notes: The XML's IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID values all identify CME00007.

The source XML contains two Middle English DIV2 witness streams followed by Kribel's Latin comparison as a third DIV2; the wrapper label versions of text is therefore edition-structural rather than a census of three medieval witnesses.

rights.basis: The artifact represents the U-M item whose provider asserts No Copyright - United States; no separate rights statement was found in the local wrapper.
rights.component: textual content of the local CME00007 XML snapshot

access.accessMethod: Open the U-M item page and select Entire text or follow the persistent UMDL handle.
access.entityLabel: University of Michigan CME item CME00007

rights.basis: The U-M item and collection pages label the material public domain and apply the No Copyright - United States rights statement.
rights.component: U-M electronic text and item content as supplied by the provider
notes: This records U-M's provider assertion; it does not grant rights in independent manuscript photographs or modern scholarship linked from this manifest.

entities.description: A publicly viewable electronic text of the textual portion of Kribel's article. It serializes the printed A and B columns as consecutive Middle English divisions, then serializes the Latin page-foot comparison as a further division, while preserving printed-page milestones, notes, and italic expansions.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME item CME00007
notes: The U-M collection page describes its historical editions as manually keyed with in-house proofing and editing.
The item display lists Richard Rolle among authors because it reproduces edition-level legacy metadata; this is not accepted work-level authorship evidence.
A 2016 revision record says the item was checked, restructured, and validated; it does not establish the date of the original electronic publication.

rights.basis: The U-M item and collection pages label the material public domain and apply the No Copyright - United States rights statement.
rights.component: U-M electronic text and item content as supplied by the provider
notes: This records U-M's provider assertion; it does not grant rights in independent manuscript photographs or modern scholarship linked from this manifest.

bibliographic.citation: Gustav Kribel, 'Studien zu Richard Rolle de Hampole. II. Lamentatio St. Bernardi de compassione Mariae,' Englische Studien 8 (1885), 67-114.
bibliographic.title: Studien zu Richard Rolle de Hampole. II. Lamentatio St. Bernardi de compassione Mariae

entities.description: The immediate print source of CME00007: a German study followed by a parallel, non-normalized transcription of Vernon (A) and CUL Dd.1.1 (B), with a Latin pseudo-Bernard comparison at the foot of the pages and editorial notes. The article introduction occupies pp. 67-84; the portion encoded by U-M occupies pp. 85-114.
entities.label: Kribel's 1885 parallel edition
notes: Kribel says this article revises the partial form printed in his 1883 Breslau dissertation.
He rejects the Cambridge catalogue's Richard Rolle conjecture on dialectal grounds and concludes that the author cannot be identified.
His Latin comparison was made from an Antwerp 1616 printing; its exact bibliographic identity remains unresolved and it must not be treated as the medieval translator's physical exemplar.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1885 and is in the public domain in the United States by age.
rights.component: Kribel's 1885 transcription, introduction, notes, and editorial arrangement

entities.description: A 1965 photographic reprint of the 1885 journal volume. The Internet Archive scan used here images this reprint copy rather than an original 1885 printing.
entities.label: Johnson Reprint Corporation photographic reprint of Englische Studien 8

rights.basis: The scanned object is a 1965 reprint rather than an 1885 original. No renewal, registration, or reprint-rights investigation was completed, and the public-domain status of the underlying article does not decide rights in new 1965 matter.
rights.component: 1965 photographic reprint edition and any new front matter

access.accessMethod: Download the complete PDF scan directly or browse the Internet Archive item.
access.entityLabel: Internet Archive scan of the 1965 Englische Studien 8 reprint

localCopies.label: Complete PDF scan of the 1965 reprint of Englische Studien 8
notes: The scanned physical object is Johnson Reprint Corporation's 1965 photographic reprint, as shown by its title and reprint leaves.

workPortion.label: The exact Kribel pages represented by CME00007
notes: Kribel's full article begins at printed p. 67 / PDF p. 77 / leaf n76; U-M omits the German study on pp. 67-84.

rights.basis: The item metadata did not expose an item-level license or rights field. Rights in the underlying 1885 article, the 1965 reprint layer, and the digital scan are therefore recorded separately.
rights.component: Internet Archive digital scan files and scan-specific metadata

entities.description: A complete 522-page PDF scan of the 1965 photographic reprint. Kribel's article is at PDF pages 77-124; the exact portion represented by CME00007 is printed pp. 85-114, PDF pages 95-124.
entities.label: Internet Archive scan of the 1965 Englische Studien 8 reprint

rights.basis: The item metadata did not expose an item-level license or rights field. Rights in the underlying 1885 article, the 1965 reprint layer, and the digital scan are therefore recorded separately.
rights.component: Internet Archive digital scan files and scan-specific metadata

access.accessMethod: Open the official Western Medieval Manuscripts catalogue record.
access.entityLabel: Bodleian Library MS. Eng. poet. a. 1

entities.description: One of Kribel's two directly transcribed witnesses. The poem occupies ff. 287rc-288vc in the main codex and is complete in 736 lines in Kribel's transcription.

holding.notes: Digital Bodleian provides a complete 710-canvas color surrogate.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Eng. poet. a. 1

physicalDescription.extent: viii + 342 + i surviving leaves, incomplete; originally viii + 413. The poem occupies ff. 287rc-288vc.
physicalDescription.format: Large religious and didactic miscellany; the target surfaces use three columns
notes: The Bodleian record gives England, West Midlands? as origin.
The work begins in column c of fol. 287r and ends in column c of fol. 288v; the last surface also begins the next item.

rights.basis: The work and all known manuscript attestations predate modern copyright by centuries. This assessment does not extend to photographs, editorial matter, transcriptions, database structure, or provider terms.
rights.component: underlying anonymous Middle English work text, considered independently of any manuscript photograph, transcription, edition, or encoding

access.accessMethod: Browse the complete Digital Bodleian object or consume its IIIF Presentation manifest.

access.format: IIIF color page images and Presentation manifest

localCopies.label: Complete 710-canvas Vernon Manuscript IIIF bundle
notes: Every canvas was requested at a 1600-pixel width; the bundle inventory preserves the exact request and native full-size fallback URL for each image.
Complete means that every public canvas in the full physical-object manifest is represented, not that the ZIP contains preservation-master resolution.

workPortion.label: Lamentation of Mary to Saint Bernard in the full Vernon Manuscript
notes: The poem starts in column c on fol. 287r and ends in column c on fol. 288v; the final canvas also begins the following work.

localCopies.label: Original Vernon Manuscript IIIF Presentation manifest

notes: The public image license is noncommercial and does not follow from the public-domain status of the medieval text.

rights.basis: The Digital Bodleian manifest attributes the images to the Bodleian Libraries and supplies a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 license; Bodleian reproduction guidance governs publication uses.
rights.component: Digital Bodleian manuscript photographs and IIIF image derivatives
notes: The license allows reuse only within its terms; commercial repository or print-on-demand uses require separate review or permission.
Local caching for research does not itself authorize redistribution of the cached ZIP.

entities.description: A complete color digital surrogate of the physical codex, including covers, flyleaves, all surviving manuscript leaves, and the four canvases carrying this poem.
entities.label: Digital Bodleian facsimile of the Vernon Manuscript
notes: The cached bundle covers the complete Digital Bodleian physical object, not only ff. 287r-288v.
The work portion is mapped to canvases 460-463, with stable start and end canvas URLs recorded in access metadata.

rights.basis: The Digital Bodleian manifest attributes the images to the Bodleian Libraries and supplies a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 license; Bodleian reproduction guidance governs publication uses.
rights.component: Digital Bodleian manuscript photographs and IIIF image derivatives
notes: The license allows reuse only within its terms; commercial repository or print-on-demand uses require separate review or permission.

access.accessMethod: No public complete manuscript facsimile was located in CUL Digital Library or public catalog searches.
notes: The historical catalogue scan is not a facsimile of the manuscript.

access.accessMethod: Submit an order through CUL Imaging Services, specifying MS Dd.1.1 and requesting the complete physical codex; identify ff. 21r-29v as the work portion.
access.contact: Use the CUL Imaging Services online order form.
notes: CUL's terms distinguish private research supply from publication and restrict sharing or uploading supplied private copies without separate permission.
Ask for the whole manuscript first; the target span for association is ff. 21r-29v, with fol. 26 missing.

dateStatements.label: catalogue dating
dateStatements.value: latter half of the fourteenth century

dateStatements.label: Kribel's dating
dateStatements.notes: Retained separately because it conflicts with the older CUL catalogue dating.

entities.description: Kribel's second directly transcribed witness. The poem occupies ff. 21r-29v, corresponding to manuscript pages 29-42 in the 1856 catalogue; fol. 26 was already missing, and the surviving text is imperfect.

holding.notes: No public complete digital facsimile was found. CUL Imaging Services accepts image orders when exact shelfmark and folios are supplied.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Dd.1.1

physicalDescription.extent: Originally paginated to 552; now mutilated. The poem occupies ff. 21r-29v / manuscript pp. 29-42, with fol. 26 missing.
physicalDescription.format: Miscellany, averaging about 50 lines per page
notes: The 1856 catalogue calls the codex mutilated and gives the poem's rubric, incipit, explicit, and page span.
DIMEV associates this witness with LALME linguistic profile 558, Isle of Ely.

access.accessMethod: The exact-shelfmark Digital Bodleian API result is partial and exposes only f. 115r; no complete public object was found.
Contact Bodleian Imaging Services and request the complete physical codex, naming ff. 159v-164rb as the title-and-work span.
notes: The published minimum charge is £20, but a full-codex request may be substantially more expensive and must be quoted.
Publication or repository redistribution requires separate attention to Bodleian licensing terms.

entities.description: A collateral witness recorded by DIMEV, not used in Kribel's edition. The poem is at ff. 160ra-164rb, with its title at f. 159v.

holding.notes: Digital Bodleian exposes only a selected image of f. 115r, outside the poem; complete imaging may be requested from Bodleian Imaging Services.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Laud Misc. 463

physicalDescription.extent: The poem occupies ff. 160ra-164rb; its title is at f. 159v.
notes: DIMEV associates this witness with LALME linguistic profile 7750, Worcestershire.

access.accessMethod: Open the official Gameson catalogue PDF in a browser.
notes: The PDF is publicly indexed and browser-readable, but the automated download attempt encountered a JavaScript proof-of-work challenge; no local copy is claimed.

access.accessMethod: No complete public page-image source was found in the Trinity special-collections or Oxford manuscript records.
Use Trinity College's special-collections contact route to ask whether complete-codex photography can be supplied; identify ff. 167r-169v as the target work span.
access.format: enquiry for digital manuscript photography or onsite consultation

dateStatements.label: manuscript production
dateStatements.value: last quarter of the fourteenth century, after 1375

entities.description: A collateral witness recorded by DIMEV and later collated by Horstmann. The poem is the final item, ff. 167r-169v, and ends imperfectly at the end of the codex; the modern catalogue estimates that about five pages are missing.

holding.notes: The Trinity manuscripts are administered through the college's special-collections arrangements at the Bodleian Weston Library. No public complete facsimile was found.

entities.label: Trinity College Oxford MS 57

physicalDescription.extent: 169 leaves plus flyleaves; incomplete at beginning and end. The poem occupies ff. 167r-169v.
notes: The catalogue places production in southern England.
DIMEV associates this witness with LALME linguistic profile 5850, Sussex.

access.accessMethod: Open the British Library Archives and Manuscripts catalogue record.
The current official catalog record has no Digitised Content link, and no complete public manuscript facsimile was located.
Request a quotation through the BL digitisation service, specifying Add MS 11307, asking first for the complete codex, and identifying ff. 97v-117v as the work span.
access.contact: Use the digitisation quotation form; contact the licensing team separately for publication permission.
notes: The service asks for shelfmark, title, and exact pages or folios; original manuscripts are photographed rather than scanned.
Image supply does not itself grant publication or repository redistribution rights.

entities.description: A collateral fifteenth-century vellum witness recorded by DIMEV, at ff. 97v-117v, with facing modern transcripts by Joseph Haslewood. It was not used in Kribel's edition.

holding.notes: The current catalog record has no Digitised Content link. The BL digitisation service accepts quotation requests with exact shelfmark and folio details.

entities.label: British Library Add MS 11307

notes: DIMEV associates this witness with LALME linguistic profile 7890, Warwickshire.
The BL catalogue describes a four-line-stanza dialogue; that description and DIMEV's general eight-line abababab work-level analysis should be retained at their respective descriptive layers.

bibliographic.citation: Carl Horstmann, ed., The Minor Poems of the Vernon MS, part 1, EETS Original Series 98 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1892), 297-328.
bibliographic.container_title: Early English Text Society, Original Series
bibliographic.title: The Minor Poems of the Vernon MS, Part I

entities.description: A later edition based on the Vernon Manuscript, with variants from CUL Dd.1.1, Trinity College Oxford MS 57, and an unidentified source called MS. L. 70. It is a comparison source, not an ancestor of CME00007.
entities.label: Horstmann's 1892 Vernon edition
notes: Horstmann attributes the poem to Richard Maidstone without giving reasoning accepted by the current DIMEV record.
The identity of MS. L. 70 was not established and it does not appear in DIMEV's current six-witness census.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1892 and is in the public domain in the United States by age.
rights.component: Horstmann's 1892 transcription, variants, notes, and editorial arrangement

entities.description: A witness siglum used in Horstmann's 1892 apparatus. Its institution, current shelfmark, extent, survival, and relationship to DIMEV's six known witnesses have not been established.
entities.label: Unidentified manuscript designated MS. L. 70 by Horstmann

access.accessMethod: Kribel's place-and-year reference was verified, but the exact edition and a matching public digital surrogate were not identified.
notes: A later Antwerp Opera Omnia was found during research but was not substituted for Kribel's explicitly dated 1616 source.

dateStatements.label: publication
dateStatements.notes: The place and year are Kribel's explicit statement; the exact bibliographic object remains unidentified.

entities.description: The early-modern Latin printing that Kribel says he used for his page-foot comparison. The present research did not establish an exact title, printer, or surviving digital copy, and no later Antwerp edition has been substituted for it.
entities.label: Unresolved Antwerp 1616 Latin pseudo-Bernard printing

locators: witness and source discussion around p. 134

bibliographic.title: Maria Mediatrix: Mediating the Divine in the Devotional Literature of Late Medieval and Early Modern England

entities.description: A modern thesis used as a supporting scholarly description of the six-witness census, affective-devotional context, and proposed Anglo-Norman intermediary. It is not an ancestor of CME00007.

rights.basis: A modern thesis remains protected absent a broader license; public access through Library and Archives Canada does not place it in the public domain or authorize substantial republication.

next_steps: Request quotations for the complete physical codices, not only the target leaves.
For Laud Misc. 463 identify ff. 159v-164rb as the title-and-work span; for Douce 126 identify ff. 84v-90.
Ask explicitly about repository redistribution, noncommercial reference display, and commercial print-on-demand research context before publishing any supplied images.
notes: The currently public one-image objects are outside both work spans and are not substitutes for complete facsimiles.
openQuestions.question: Can complete color digital facsimiles of Bodleian MSS Laud Misc. 463 and Douce 126 be obtained and licensed for the project's intended reference use?

next_steps: Ask each holding institution first for complete-codex photography and a quotation.
Associate the work respectively with Dd.1.1 ff. 21r-29v, Trinity 57 ff. 167r-169v, and Add MS 11307 ff. 97v-117v.
Record any supply contract and publication permission separately from copyright in the underlying medieval text.
openQuestions.question: Can complete color digital facsimiles be obtained for CUL MS Dd.1.1, Trinity College Oxford MS 57, and BL Add MS 11307?

next_steps: Trace Horstmann's source notes, sigla in adjacent EETS editions, and contemporary library catalogues.
Do not add L. 70 to the current six-witness census until it is identified or shown to duplicate a known witness.
openQuestions.question: What physical manuscript did Horstmann mean by MS. L. 70?

next_steps: Search historical Bernardine bibliographies and Antwerp printer catalogues for a 1616 edition containing the relevant Quis dabit text.
Compare title-page and passage evidence before linking any candidate; do not substitute the discoverable 1620 Antwerp Opera Omnia merely because it is similar.
openQuestions.question: Which exact Antwerp 1616 pseudo-Bernard edition supplied Kribel's Latin comparison?

next_steps: Inspect the reprint's copyright notice and determine whether a relevant U.S. renewal or registration exists.
Prefer an original 1885 scan for redistribution if one becomes available, while retaining this full scan for private source comparison.
openQuestions.question: What rights, if any, subsist in Johnson Reprint Corporation's 1965 photographic reprint and its new matter?

next_steps: Consult C. W. Marx's full studies and subsequent work on the Quis dabit tradition.
Compare all six Middle English witnesses against the proposed Anglo-Norman intermediary and Latin pseudo-Bernard tradition.
Keep Kribel's early-modern comparison edition distinct from any inferred medieval exemplar.
openQuestions.question: What exact Latin and/or Anglo-Norman textual route underlies the Middle English Lamentation?

reviewNotes: The source graph distinguishes the anonymous Middle English work, six physical witnesses, nineteenth-century editions, modern reproductions, U-M's encoding, and this repository artifact.

Only the Vernon and Cambridge Dd.1.1 witness texts feed Kribel's 1885 edition and therefore CME00007. The four witnesses added by the modern DIMEV census are collateral witnesses, not direct ancestors of the encoded text.

The complete Vernon digital object was cached rather than only the four target canvases. The exact work portion is ff. 287rc-288vc, IIIF canvases 460-463; the cached 1600-pixel bundle is complete in surface coverage, not a preservation master.

The public Digital Bodleian records for Laud Misc. 463 and Douce 126 contain only one selected surface each, neither carrying this poem. Their cached manifests are metadata-only supporting records and must not be presented as work facsimiles.

Rights for the medieval text, editorial matter, reprints, digital scans, manuscript photography, datasets, and requested future images are separate layers and are not inherited through lineage relations.

lineage.summary: The repository XML represents University of Michigan CME item CME00007, a keyed encoding of Gustav Kribel's 1885 parallel edition. Kribel transcribed two Middle English witnesses: the Vernon Manuscript, Bodleian Library MS. Eng. poet. a. 1, ff. 287rc-288vc, and Cambridge University Library MS Dd.1.1, ff. 21r-29v. His Latin page-foot comparison is an editorial source layer, not a third Middle English witness. The current DIMEV census records four additional witnesses that are not ancestors of CME00007: Bodleian MSS Laud Misc. 463 and Douce 126, Trinity College Oxford MS 57, and British Library Add MS 11307. A complete 710-canvas color surrogate of the Vernon Manuscript is cached, with the work mapped to canvases 460-463 inside the full codex. No complete public digital facsimile was found for the other five witnesses; only unrelated selected surfaces are public for Laud Misc. 463 and Douce 126, and institutional request routes are recorded separately.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: By the late fourteenth century; exact composition date unknown
catalogSummary.region: England; Kribel tentatively reconstructed a southern Midlands dialect near Kent, but surviving witness dialects are geographically diverse
catalogSummary.title: Lamentation of Mary to Saint Bernard

catalogingSubject.label: Lamentation of Mary to Saint Bernard
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous late-fourteenth-century Middle English stanzaic dialogue commonly called the Lamentation of Mary to Saint Bernard. The subject is one intellectual work represented by six witnesses in the current DIMEV census. It excludes those physical witnesses, Kribel's 1885 parallel-text edition and Latin comparison, the U-M electronic encoding, and the repository XML.

contentParts.label: Lamentation of Mary to Saint Bernard

locators.value: ff. 21r-29v; manuscript pages 29-42 in the 1856 catalogue

notes: The six witness texts are versions of this one work, not intrinsic sequential parts.
contentParts.summary: The complete dialogue as transmitted with variant ordering, additions, omissions, and endings across six witnesses.

assertion.notes: Late-fourteenth-century copies establish a terminus ante quem; the lower bound is deliberately broad.

dateStatements.display: By the late fourteenth century; exact date unknown

assertion.notes: This identifies an earliest surviving horizon, not necessarily one uniquely earliest codex.

dateStatements.display: Late fourteenth century; Trinity College Oxford MS 57 was copied after 1375, while the Vernon and Laud witnesses also date to the late fourteenth century

extent.description: One poem whose extent varies by witness. Kribel prints 91 stanza groups and 736 lines for Vernon (A), and 96 stanza groups and 764 lines for Cambridge Dd.1.1 (B). The repository XML therefore contains 187 LG and 1,500 L elements across two parallel witness streams, not a 1,500-line authored work; its additional Latin division contains one prose stream.

formStatements.description: Four-stress lines in eight-line stanzas rhyming abababab; witness length and stanza count vary.

assertion.notes: Kribel's complete Latin comparison stream is an edition layer rather than a co-primary language of the Middle English work.

notes: DIMEV's six-witness census supersedes Kribel's statement that the poem survives in only two manuscripts; Kribel's two are the only direct Middle English sources of CME00007.
Kribel's lost common exemplar is a stemmatic inference for Vernon and CUL Dd.1.1, not a surviving manuscript entity or a license to infer a single canonical line count.
Rights for the medieval work, nineteenth-century editions, U-M encoding, modern scans, and manuscript photographs are kept separate in the lineage manifest.

next_steps: Consult C. W. Marx's full 1990 chapter and later linguistic scholarship comparing all witnesses.
Keep work origin distinct from LALME localizations of individual copyists.
openQuestions.question: Can the poem's composition date and dialect region be established more precisely from all six witnesses?

next_steps: Consult Marx's 1990 and 1994 studies and compare their source analysis with Kribel's Antwerp-1616 collation.
Do not treat Kribel's early-modern Latin printing as the medieval translator's physical exemplar.
openQuestions.question: What exact Latin and/or Anglo-Norman textual route underlies this Middle English version?

next_steps: Review Horstmann's unpublished notes or later attribution scholarship.
Continue to catalog the work as anonymous unless reliable evidence supersedes DIMEV and Kribel.
openQuestions.question: What evidence, if any, led Horstmann to attribute the poem to Richard Maidstone?

assertion.notes: DIMEV/LALME localizes individual witness hands to Worcestershire, Essex, Sussex, Ely, and Warwickshire; those localizations must not be averaged into a work place.

scope.description: Kribel's reconstructed language of the shared earlier text, not the surface dialect of any one surviving copy

assertion.notes: The current DIMEV record supplies no named author, and Kribel concludes that no evidence remains to identify one.
Recorded as reception history, not accepted authorship. The 1856 catalogue conjectured Rolle from the hermit stanza; Kribel rejects the conjecture.
Horstmann asserts the attribution while citing Kribel, but Kribel names no alternative author and DIMEV does not repeat Maidstone's name.

scope.description: The two-witness parallel edition and Latin comparison represented by CME00007

summaries.value: A stanzaic Middle English devotional poem in which Saint Bernard asks the Virgin Mary to recount Christ's Passion. Mary narrates arrest, trial, crucifixion, descent from the cross, burial, and her own grief; the dialogue teaches readers compassionate meditation on Christ's suffering through Mary's testimony.

CME00007.xml does not encode three medieval versions. It serializes Kribel's two parallel Middle English witness transcriptions as consecutive divisions and then serializes his page-foot Latin source comparison as a third 'version'. The Latin comparison and German notes belong to the 1885 edition layer.

assertion.notes: A normalized discovery title following modern scholarship; no single authorial title is established across all six witnesses.

titles.value: Lamentation of Mary to Saint Bernard

assertion.notes: Normalized from DIMEV's name field; witness spellings differ.

titles.value: Lewd men be not lered in lore / As clerks been in holy writ

scope.description: Rubric and rhyming heading in the Vernon witness

titles.value: Her is a gret lamentacion be twene vr ladi & seint Bernard / Of cristes passion hire dere sone þat was so pyneful & so hard

scope.description: Rubric in Cambridge University Library MS Dd.1.1

titles.value: Lamentacio sancti Bernardi de compassione beate Marie virginis ex dulcissimi filii sui passione et eiusdem crudeli morte

notes: This names Kribel's article and should not be mistaken for a medieval authorial title.
titles.value: Studien zu Richard Rolle de Hampole. II. Lamentatio St. Bernardi de compassione Mariae [Englische Studien 8 (1885)]
